NKJV.1: Do  not boast about tomorrow, For you do not know what a day may bring forth.
NKJV.2: Let another man praise you, and not your own mouth; A stranger, and not your own lips.
NKJV.3: A stone is heavy and sand is weighty, But a fool’s wrath is heavier than both of them.
NKJV.4: Wrath is cruel and anger a torrent, But who is able to stand before jealousy?
NKJV.5: Open rebuke is better Than love carefully concealed.
NKJV.6: Faithful are the wounds of a friend, But the kisses of an enemy are deceitful.
NKJV.7: A satisfied soul loathes the honeycomb, But to a hungry soul every bitter thing is sweet.
NKJV.8: Like a bird that wanders from its nest Is a man who wanders from his place.
NKJV.9: Ointment and perfume delight the heart, And the sweetness of a man’s friend gives delight by hearty counsel.
NKJV.10: Do not forsake your own friend or your father’s friend, Nor go to your brother’s house in the day of your calamity; Better is a neighbor nearby than a brother far away.
NKJV.11: My son, be wise, and make my heart glad,  That I may answer him who reproaches me.
NKJV.12: A prudent man foresees evil and hides himself; The simple pass on and are punished.
NKJV.13: Take the garment of him who is surety for a stranger, And hold it in pledge when he is surety for a seductress.
NKJV.14: He who blesses his friend with a loud voice, rising early in the morning, It will be counted a curse to him.
NKJV.15: A continual dripping on a very rainy day And a contentious woman are alike;
NKJV.16: Whoever restrains her restrains the wind, And grasps oil with his right hand.
NKJV.17: As iron sharpens iron, So a man sharpens the countenance of his friend.
NKJV.18: Whoever keeps the fig tree will eat its fruit; So he who waits on his master will be honored.
NKJV.19: As in water face reflects face, So a man’s heart reveals the man.
NKJV.20: Hell and Destruction are never full; So the eyes of man are never satisfied.
NKJV.21: The refining pot is for silver and the furnace for gold, And a man is valued by what others say of him.
NKJV.22: Though you grind a fool in a mortar with a pestle along with crushed grain, Yet his foolishness will not depart from him.
NKJV.23: Be diligent to know the state of your flocks, And attend to your herds;
NKJV.24: For riches are not forever, Nor does a crown endure to all generations.
NKJV.25: When the hay is removed, and the tender grass shows itself, And the herbs of the mountains are gathered in,
NKJV.26: The lambs will provide your clothing, And the goats the price of a field;
NKJV.27: You shall have enough goats’ milk for your food, For the food of your household, And the nourishment of your maidservants.

KJV.1: Boast<S>1984</S> not thyself of to morrow<S>4279</S>; for thou knowest<S>3045</S> not what a day<S>3117</S> may bring forth<S>3205</S>. <sup>to: Heb. to morrow day</sup>

ESV.1: Do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what a day may bring.
ESV.2: Let another praise you, and not your own mouth; a stranger, and not your own lips.
ESV.3: A stone is heavy, and sand is weighty, but a fool's provocation is heavier than both.
ESV.4: Wrath is cruel, anger is overwhelming, but who can stand before jealousy?
ESV.5: Better is open rebuke than hidden love.
ESV.6: Faithful are the wounds of a friend; profuse are the kisses of an enemy.
ESV.7: One who is full loathes honey, but to one who is hungry everything bitter is sweet.
ESV.8: Like a bird that strays from its nest is a man who strays from his home.
ESV.9: Oil and perfume make the heart glad, and the sweetness of a friend comes from his earnest counsel.
ESV.10: Do not forsake your friend and your father's friend, and do not go to your brother's house in the day of your calamity. Better is a neighbor who is near than a brother who is far away.
ESV.11: Be wise, my son, and make my heart glad, that I may answer him who reproaches me.
ESV.12: The prudent sees danger and hides himself, but the simple go on and suffer for it.
ESV.13: Take a man's garment when he has put up security for a stranger, and hold it in pledge when he puts up security for an adulteress.
ESV.14: Whoever blesses his neighbor with a loud voice, rising early in the morning, will be counted as cursing.
ESV.15: A continual dripping on a rainy day and a quarrelsome wife are alike;
ESV.16: to restrain her is to restrain the wind or to grasp  oil in one's right hand.
ESV.17: Iron sharpens iron, and one man sharpens another.
ESV.18: Whoever tends a fig tree will eat its fruit, and he who guards his master will be honored.
ESV.19: As in water face reflects face, so the heart of man reflects the man.
ESV.20: Sheol and Abaddon are never satisfied, and never satisfied are the eyes of man.
ESV.21: The crucible is for silver, and the furnace is for gold, and a man is tested by his praise.
ESV.22: Crush a fool in a mortar with a pestle along with crushed grain, yet his folly will not depart from him.
ESV.23: Know well the condition of your flocks, and give attention to your herds,
ESV.24: for riches do not last forever; and does a crown endure to all generations?
ESV.25: When the grass is gone and the new growth appears and the vegetation of the mountains is gathered,
ESV.26: the lambs will provide your clothing, and the goats the price of a field.
ESV.27: There will be enough goats' milk for your food, for the food of your household and maintenance for your girls.

NASB.1: Do not boast about tomorrow, For you do not know what a day may bring forth.
NASB.2: Let another praise you, and not your own mouth; A stranger, and not your own lips.
NASB.3: A stone is heavy and the sand weighty, But the provocation of a fool is heavier than both of them.
NASB.4: Wrath is fierce and anger is a flood, But who can stand before jealousy?
NASB.5: Better is open rebuke Than love that is concealed.
NASB.6: Faithful are the wounds of a friend, But deceitful are the kisses of an enemy.
NASB.7: A sated man loathes honey, But to a famished man any bitter thing is sweet.
NASB.8: Like a bird that wanders from her nest, So is a man who wanders from his home.
NASB.9: Oil and perfume make the heart glad, So a man's counsel is sweet to his friend.
NASB.10: Do not forsake your own friend or your father's friend, And do not go to your brother's house in the day of your calamity; Better is a neighbor who is near than a brother far away.
NASB.11: Be wise, my son, and make my heart glad, That I may reply to him who reproaches me.
NASB.12: A prudent man sees evil [and] hides himself, The naive proceed [and] pay the penalty.
NASB.13: Take his garment when he becomes surety for a stranger; And for an adulterous woman hold him in pledge.
NASB.14: He who blesses his friend with a loud voice early in the morning, It will be reckoned a curse to him.
NASB.15: A constant dripping on a day of steady rain And a contentious woman are alike;
NASB.16: He who would restrain her restrains the wind, And grasps oil with his right hand.
NASB.17: Iron sharpens iron, So one man sharpens another.
NASB.18: He who tends the fig tree will eat its fruit, And he who cares for his master will be honored.
NASB.19: As in water face [reflects] face, So the heart of man [reflects] man.
NASB.20: Sheol and Abaddon are never satisfied, Nor are the eyes of man ever satisfied.
NASB.21: The crucible is for silver and the furnace for gold, And each [is tested] by the praise accorded him.
NASB.22: Though you pound a fool in a mortar with a pestle along with crushed grain, [Yet] his foolishness will not depart from him.
NASB.23: Know well the condition of your flocks, [And] pay attention to your herds;
NASB.24: For riches are not forever, Nor does a crown [endure] to all generations.
NASB.25: [When] the grass disappears, the new growth is seen, And the herbs of the mountains are gathered in,
NASB.26: The lambs [will be] for your clothing, And the goats [will bring] the price of a field,
NASB.27: And [there will be] goats' milk enough for your food, For the food of your household, And sustenance for your maidens.

NLT.1:
Don’t brag about tomorrow,
since you don’t know what the day will bring.
NLT.2:
Let someone else praise you, not your own mouth —
a stranger, not your own lips.
NLT.3:
A stone is heavy and sand is weighty,
but the resentment caused by a fool is even heavier.
NLT.4:
Anger is cruel, and wrath is like a flood,
but jealousy is even more dangerous.
NLT.5:
An open rebuke
is better than hidden love!
NLT.6:
Wounds from a sincere friend
are better than many kisses from an enemy.
NLT.7:
A person who is full refuses honey,
but even bitter food tastes sweet to the hungry.
NLT.8:
A person who strays from home
is like a bird that strays from its nest.
NLT.9:
The heartfelt counsel of a friend
is as sweet as perfume and incense.
NLT.10:
Never abandon a friend —
either yours or your father’s.
When disaster strikes, you won’t have to ask your brother for assistance.
It’s better to go to a neighbor than to a brother who lives far away.
NLT.11:
Be wise, my child, and make my heart glad.
Then I will be able to answer my critics.
NLT.12:
A prudent person foresees danger and takes precautions.
The simpleton goes blindly on and suffers the consequences.
NLT.13:
Get security from someone who guarantees a stranger’s debt.
Get a deposit if he does it for foreigners.
NLT.14:
A loud and cheerful greeting early in the morning
will be taken as a curse!
NLT.15:
A quarrelsome wife is as annoying
as constant dripping on a rainy day.
NLT.16:
Stopping her complaints is like trying to stop the wind
or trying to hold something with greased hands.
NLT.17:
As iron sharpens iron,
so a friend sharpens a friend.
NLT.18:
As workers who tend a fig tree are allowed to eat the fruit,
so workers who protect their employer’s interests will be rewarded.
NLT.19:
As a face is reflected in water,
so the heart reflects the real person.
NLT.20:
Just as Death and Destruction are never satisfied,
so human desire is never satisfied.
NLT.21:
Fire tests the purity of silver and gold,
but a person is tested by being praised.
NLT.22:
You cannot separate fools from their foolishness,
even though you grind them like grain with mortar and pestle.
NLT.23:
Know the state of your flocks,
and put your heart into caring for your herds,
NLT.24:
for riches don’t last forever,
and the crown might not be passed to the next generation.
NLT.25:
After the hay is harvested and the new crop appears
and the mountain grasses are gathered in,
NLT.26:
your sheep will provide wool for clothing,
and your goats will provide the price of a field.
NLT.27:
And you will have enough goats’ milk for yourself,
your family, and your servant girls.

WEB.1: Don’t boast about tomorrow; for you don’t know what a day may bring.
WEB.2: Let another man praise you, and not your own mouth; a stranger, and not your own lips.
WEB.3: A stone is heavy, and sand is a burden; but a fool’s provocation is heavier than both.
WEB.4: Wrath is cruel, and anger is overwhelming; but who is able to stand before jealousy?
WEB.5: Better is open rebuke than hidden love.
WEB.6: Faithful are the wounds of a friend; although the kisses of an enemy are profuse.
WEB.7: A full soul loathes a honeycomb; but to a hungry soul, every bitter thing is sweet.
WEB.8: As a bird that wanders from her nest, so is a man who wanders from his home.
WEB.9: Perfume and incense bring joy to the heart; so does earnest counsel from a man’s friend.
WEB.10: Don’t forsake your friend and your father’s friend. Don’t go to your brother’s house in the day of your disaster: better is a neighbor who is near than a distant brother.
WEB.11: Be wise, my son, and bring joy to my heart, then I can answer my tormentor.
WEB.12: A prudent man sees danger and takes refuge; but the simple pass on, and suffer for it.
WEB.13: Take his garment when he puts up collateral for a stranger. Hold it for a wayward woman!
WEB.14: He who blesses his neighbor with a loud voice early in the morning, it will be taken as a curse by him.
WEB.15: A continual dropping on a rainy day and a contentious wife are alike:
WEB.16: restraining her is like restraining the wind, or like grasping oil in his right hand.
WEB.17: Iron sharpens iron; so a man sharpens his friend’s countenance.
WEB.18: Whoever tends the fig tree shall eat its fruit. He who looks after his master shall be honored.
WEB.19: Like water reflects a face, so a man’s heart reflects the man.
WEB.20: Sheol  and Abaddon are never satisfied; and a man’s eyes are never satisfied.
WEB.21: The crucible is for silver, and the furnace for gold; but man is refined by his praise.
WEB.22: Though you grind a fool in a mortar with a pestle along with grain, yet his foolishness will not be removed from him.
WEB.23: Know well the state of your flocks, and pay attention to your herds:
WEB.24: for riches are not forever, nor does the crown endure to all generations.
WEB.25: The hay is removed, and the new growth appears, the grasses of the hills are gathered in.
WEB.26: The lambs are for your clothing, and the goats are the price of a field.
WEB.27: There will be plenty of goats’ milk for your food, for your family’s food, and for the nourishment of your servant girls.

CSB17.1:
Don’t boast about tomorrow,
for you don’t know what a day might bring. ⓛ
CSB17.2:
Let another praise you, and not your own mouth —
a stranger, and not your own lips. ⓜ
CSB17.3:
A stone is heavy and sand, a burden,
but aggravation from a fool outweighs them both.
CSB17.4:
Fury is cruel, and anger a flood,
but who can withstand jealousy? ⓝ
CSB17.5:
Better an open reprimand
than concealed love. ⓐ
CSB17.6:
The wounds of a friend are trustworthy, ⓑ
but the kisses of an enemy are excessive. ⓒ
CSB17.7:
A person who is full tramples on a honeycomb, ⓓ
but to a hungry person, any bitter thing is sweet.
CSB17.8:
Anyone wandering from his home
is like a bird wandering from its nest. ⓔ
CSB17.9:
Oil ⓕ and incense bring joy to the heart,
and the sweetness of a friend is better than self-counsel. [1]
CSB17.10:
Don’t abandon your friend or your father’s friend, ⓖ
and don’t go to your brother’s house
in your time of calamity;
better a neighbor nearby than a brother far away. ⓗ
CSB17.11:
Be wise, my son, and bring my heart joy, ⓘ
so that I can answer anyone who taunts me. ⓙ
CSB17.12:
A sensible person sees danger and takes cover;
the inexperienced keep going and are punished. ⓚ
CSB17.13:
Take his garment,
for he has put up security for a stranger;
get collateral if it is for foreigners. [2] ⓛ
CSB17.14:
If one blesses his neighbor
with a loud voice early in the morning,
it will be counted as a curse to him.
CSB17.15:
An endless dripping on a rainy day
and a nagging wife are alike; ⓜ
CSB17.16:
the one who controls her controls the wind
and grasps oil with his right hand.
CSB17.17:
Iron sharpens iron,
and one person sharpens another. [3]
CSB17.18:
Whoever tends a fig tree ⓝ will eat its fruit, ⓐ
and whoever looks after his master will be honored.
CSB17.19:
As water reflects the face,
so the heart reflects the person.
CSB17.20:
Sheol and Abaddon ⓑ are never satisfied, ⓒ
and people’s eyes are never satisfied. ⓓ
CSB17.21:
A crucible refines silver, a smelter refines gold,
and a person refines his praise. ⓔ
CSB17.22:
Though you grind a fool
in a mortar with a pestle along with grain,
you will not separate his foolishness from him. ⓕ
CSB17.23:
Know well the condition of your flock, ⓖ
and pay attention to your herds,
CSB17.24:
for wealth is not forever; ⓗ
not even a crown lasts for all time.
CSB17.25:
When hay is removed and new growth appears
and the grain from the hills is gathered in,
CSB17.26:
lambs will provide your clothing,
and goats, the price of a field;
CSB17.27:
there will be enough goat’s milk for your food —
food for your household
and nourishment for your female servants. ⓘ

AMP.1:
Do not boast about tomorrow,
For you do not know what a day may bring.
AMP.2:
Let another praise you, and not your own mouth;
A stranger, and not your own lips.
AMP.3:
Stone is heavy and the sand weighty,
But a fool's [unreasonable] wrath is heavier and more burdensome than both of them.
AMP.4:
Wrath is cruel and anger is an overwhelming flood,
But who is able to endure and stand before [the sin of] jealousy?
AMP.5:
Better is an open reprimand [of loving correction]
Than love that is hidden.
AMP.6:
Faithful are the wounds of a friend [who corrects out of love and concern],
But the kisses of an enemy are deceitful [because they serve his hidden agenda].
AMP.7:
He who is satisfied loathes honey,
But to the hungry soul any bitter thing is sweet.
AMP.8:
Like a bird that wanders from her nest [with its comfort and safety],
So is a man who wanders from his home.
AMP.9:
Oil and perfume make the heart glad;
So does the sweetness of a friend's counsel that comes from the heart.
AMP.10:
Do not abandon your own friend and your father's friend,
And do not go to your brother's house in the day of your disaster.
Better is a neighbor who is near than a brother who is far away.
AMP.11:
My son, be wise, and make my heart glad,
That I may reply to him who reproaches (reprimands, criticizes) me.
AMP.12:
A prudent man sees evil and hides himself and avoids it,
But the naive [who are easily misled] continue on and are punished [by suffering the consequences of sin].
AMP.13:
[The judge tells the creditor,] "Take the garment of one who is surety (guarantees a loan) for a stranger;
And hold him in pledge when he is surety for an immoral woman [for it is unlikely the debt will be repaid]."
AMP.14:
He who blesses his neighbor with a loud voice early in the morning,
It will be counted as a curse to him [for it will either be annoying or his purpose will be suspect].
AMP.15:
A constant dripping on a day of steady rain
And a contentious (quarrelsome) woman are alike;
AMP.16:
Whoever attempts to restrain her [criticism] might as well try to stop the wind,
And grasps oil with his right hand.
AMP.17:
As iron sharpens iron,
So one man sharpens [and influences] another [through discussion].
AMP.18:
He who tends the fig tree will eat its fruit,
And he who faithfully protects and cares for his master will be honored.
AMP.19:
As in water face reflects face,
So the heart of man reflects man.
AMP.20:
Sheol (the place of the dead) and Abaddon (the underworld) are never satisfied;
Nor are the eyes of man ever satisfied.
AMP.21:
The refining pot is for silver and the furnace for gold [to separate the impurities of the metal],
And each is tested by the praise given to him [and his response to it, whether humble or proud].
AMP.22:
Even though you pound a [hardened, arrogant] fool [who rejects wisdom] in a mortar with a pestle like grain,
Yet his foolishness will not leave him.
AMP.23:
Be diligent to know the condition of your flocks,
And pay attention to your herds;
AMP.24:
For riches are not forever,
Nor does a crown endure to all generations.
AMP.25:
When the grass is gone, the new growth is seen,
And herbs of the mountain are gathered in,
AMP.26:
The lambs will supply wool for your clothing,
And the goats will bring the price of a field.
AMP.27:
And there will be enough goats' milk for your food,
For the food of your household,
And for the maintenance of your maids.

MSG.1:
Don't brashly announce what you're going to do tomorrow;
you don't know the first thing about tomorrow.
MSG.2:
Don't call attention to yourself;
let others do that for you.
MSG.3:
Carrying a log across your shoulders
while you're hefting a boulder with your arms
Is nothing compared to the burden
of putting up with a fool.
MSG.4:
We're blasted by anger and swamped by rage,
but who can survive jealousy?
MSG.5:
A spoken reprimand is better
than approval that's never expressed.
MSG.6:
The wounds from a lover are worth it;
kisses from an enemy do you in.
MSG.7:
When you've stuffed yourself, you refuse dessert;
when you're starved, you could eat a horse.
MSG.8:
People who won't settle down, wandering hither and yon,
are like restless birds, flitting to and fro.
MSG.9:
Just as lotions and fragrance give sensual delight,
a sweet friendship refreshes the soul.
MSG.10:
Don't leave your friends or your parents' friends
and run home to your family when things get rough;
Better a nearby friend
than a distant family.
MSG.11:
Become wise, dear child, and make me happy;
then nothing the world throws my way will upset me.
MSG.12:
A prudent person sees trouble coming and ducks;
a simpleton walks in blindly and is clobbered.
MSG.13:
Hold tight to collateral on any loan to a stranger;
be wary of accepting what a transient has pawned.
MSG.14:
If you wake your friend in the early morning
by shouting "Rise and shine!"
It will sound to him
more like a curse than a blessing.
MSG.15:
A nagging spouse is like
the drip, drip, drip of a leaky faucet;
MSG.16:
You can't turn it off,
and you can't get away from it.
MSG.17:
You use steel to sharpen steel,
and one friend sharpens another.
MSG.18:
If you care for your orchard, you'll enjoy its fruit;
if you honor your boss, you'll be honored.
MSG.19:
Just as water mirrors your face,
so your face mirrors your heart.
MSG.20:
Hell has a voracious appetite,
and lust just never quits.
MSG.21:
The purity of silver and gold is tested
by putting them in the fire;
The purity of human hearts is tested
by giving them a little fame.
MSG.22:
Pound on a fool all you like—
you can't pound out foolishness.
MSG.23:
Know your sheep by name;
carefully attend to your flocks;
MSG.24:
(Don't take them for granted;
possessions don't last forever, you know.)
MSG.25:
And then, when the crops are in
and the harvest is stored in the barns,
MSG.26:
You can knit sweaters from lambs' wool,
and sell your goats for a profit;
MSG.27:
There will be plenty of milk and meat
to last your family through the winter.

NIV.1:
Do not boast about tomorrow,
for you do not know what a day may bring forth.
NIV.2:
Let another praise you, and not your own mouth;
someone else, and not your own lips.
NIV.3:
Stone is heavy and sand a burden,
but provocation by a fool is heavier than both.
NIV.4:
Anger is cruel and fury overwhelming,
but who can stand before jealousy?
NIV.5:
Better is open rebuke
than hidden love.
NIV.6:
Wounds from a friend can be trusted,
but an enemy multiplies kisses.
NIV.7:
He who is full loathes honey,
but to the hungry even what is bitter tastes sweet.
NIV.8:
Like a bird that strays from its nest
is a man who strays from his home.
NIV.9:
Perfume and incense bring joy to the heart,
and the pleasantness of one’s friend springs from his earnest counsel.
NIV.10:
Do not forsake your friend and the friend of your father,
and do not go to your brother’s house when disaster strikes you—
better a neighbor nearby than a brother far away.
NIV.11:
Be wise, my son, and bring joy to my heart;
then I can answer anyone who treats me with contempt.
NIV.12:
The prudent see danger and take refuge,
but the simple keep going and suffer for it.
NIV.13:
Take the garment of one who puts up security for a stranger;
hold it in pledge if he does it for a wayward woman.
NIV.14:
If a man loudly blesses his neighbor early in the morning,
it will be taken as a curse.
NIV.15:
A quarrelsome wife is like
a constant dripping on a rainy day;
NIV.16:
restraining her is like restraining the wind
or grasping oil with the hand.
NIV.17:
As iron sharpens iron,
so one man sharpens another.
NIV.18:
He who tends a fig tree will eat its fruit,
and he who looks after his master will be honored.
NIV.19:
As water reflects a face,
so a man’s heart reflects the man.
NIV.20:
Death and Destruction are never satisfied,
and neither are the eyes of man.
NIV.21:
The crucible for silver and the furnace for gold,
but man is tested by the praise he receives.
NIV.22:
Though you grind a fool in a mortar,
grinding him like grain with a pestle,
you will not remove his folly from him.
NIV.23:
Be sure you know the condition of your flocks,
give careful attention to your herds;
NIV.24:
for riches do not endure forever,
and a crown is not secure for all generations.
NIV.25:
When the hay is removed and new growth appears
and the grass from the hills is gathered in,
NIV.26:
the lambs will provide you with clothing,
and the goats with the price of a field.
NIV.27:
You will have plenty of goats’ milk
to feed you and your family
and to nourish your servant girls.

NRSVCE.1:
Do not boast about tomorrow,
for you do not know what a day may bring.
NRSVCE.2:
Let another praise you, and not your own mouth —
a stranger, and not your own lips.
NRSVCE.3:
A stone is heavy, and sand is weighty,
but a fool’s provocation is heavier than both.
NRSVCE.4:
Wrath is cruel, anger is overwhelming,
but who is able to stand before jealousy?
NRSVCE.5:
Better is open rebuke
than hidden love.
NRSVCE.6:
Well meant are the wounds a friend inflicts,
but profuse are the kisses of an enemy.
NRSVCE.7:
The sated appetite spurns honey,
but to a ravenous appetite even the bitter is sweet.
NRSVCE.8:
Like a bird that strays from its nest
is one who strays from home.
NRSVCE.9:
Perfume and incense make the heart glad,
but the soul is torn by trouble.
NRSVCE.10:
Do not forsake your friend or the friend of your parent;
do not go to the house of your kindred in the day of your calamity.
Better is a neighbor who is nearby
than kindred who are far away.
NRSVCE.11:
Be wise, my child, and make my heart glad,
so that I may answer whoever reproaches me.
NRSVCE.12:
The clever see danger and hide;
but the simple go on, and suffer for it.
NRSVCE.13:
Take the garment of one who has given surety for a stranger;
seize the pledge given as surety for foreigners.
NRSVCE.14:
Whoever blesses a neighbor with a loud voice,
rising early in the morning,
will be counted as cursing.
NRSVCE.15:
A continual dripping on a rainy day
and a contentious wife are alike;
NRSVCE.16:
to restrain her is to restrain the wind
or to grasp oil in the right hand.
NRSVCE.17:
Iron sharpens iron,
and one person sharpens the wits of another.
NRSVCE.18:
Anyone who tends a fig tree will eat its fruit,
and anyone who takes care of a master will be honored.
NRSVCE.19:
Just as water reflects the face,
so one human heart reflects another.
NRSVCE.20:
Sheol and Abaddon are never satisfied,
and human eyes are never satisfied.
NRSVCE.21:
The crucible is for silver, and the furnace is for gold,
so a person is tested by being praised.
NRSVCE.22:
Crush a fool in a mortar with a pestle
along with crushed grain,
but the folly will not be driven out.
NRSVCE.23:
Know well the condition of your flocks,
and give attention to your herds;
NRSVCE.24:
for riches do not last forever,
nor a crown for all generations.
NRSVCE.25:
When the grass is gone, and new growth appears,
and the herbage of the mountains is gathered,
NRSVCE.26:
the lambs will provide your clothing,
and the goats the price of a field;
NRSVCE.27:
there will be enough goats’ milk for your food,
for the food of your household
and nourishment for your servant-girls.

NET.1: Do not boast about tomorrow; for you do not know what a day may bring forth.
NET.2: Let another praise you, and not your own mouth; someone else, and not your own lips.
NET.3: A stone is heavy and sand is weighty, but vexation by a fool is more burdensome than the two of them.
NET.4: Wrath is cruel and anger is overwhelming, but who can stand before jealousy?
NET.5: Better is open rebuke than hidden love.
NET.6: Faithful are the wounds of a friend, but the kisses of an enemy are excessive.
NET.7: The one whose appetite is satisfied loathes honey, but to the hungry mouth every bitter thing is sweet.
NET.8: Like a bird that wanders from its nest, so is a person who wanders from his home.
NET.9: Ointment and incense make the heart rejoice, likewise the sweetness of one’s friend from sincere counsel.
NET.10: Do not forsake your friend and your father’s friend, and do not enter your brother’s house in the day of your disaster; a neighbor nearby is better than a brother far away.
NET.11: Be wise, my son, and make my heart glad, so that I may answer anyone who taunts me.
NET.12: A shrewd person sees danger and hides himself, but the naive keep right on going and suffer for it.
NET.13: Take a man’s garment when he has given security for a stranger, and when he gives surety for a stranger, hold him in pledge.
NET.14: If someone blesses his neighbor with a loud voice early in the morning, it will be counted as a curse to him.
NET.15: A continual dripping on a rainy day and a contentious wife are alike.
NET.16: Whoever hides her hides the wind or grasps oil with his right hand.
NET.17: As iron sharpens iron, so a person sharpens his friend.
NET.18: The one who tends a fig tree will eat its fruit, and whoever takes care of his master will be honored.
NET.19: As in water the face is reflected as a face, so a person’s heart reflects the person.
NET.20: As Death and Destruction are never satisfied, so the eyes of a person are never satisfied.
NET.21: As the crucible is for silver and the furnace is for gold, so a person is proved by the praise he receives.
NET.22: If you should pound the fool in the mortar among the grain with the pestle, his foolishness would not depart from him.
NET.23: Pay careful attention to the condition of your flocks, give careful attention to your herds,
NET.24: for riches do not last forever, nor does a crown last from generation to generation.
NET.25: When the hay is removed and new grass appears, and the grass from the hills is gathered in,
NET.26: the lambs will be for your clothing, and the goats will be for the price of a field.
NET.27: And there will be enough goat’s milk for your food, for the food of your household, and for the sustenance of your servant girls.
